From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Spanish name](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_name&redirect=no "Spanish name"))

This article is about naming customs in Spain. For naming customs in Spanish-speaking countries of the Americas, see [naming customs of Hispanic America](/wiki/Naming_customs_of_Hispanic_America "Naming customs of Hispanic America").

|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | |  | | José **García Torres**  *(el señor García)* | | | | | |  | | María **Acosta Gómez**  *(la señora Acosta)* | | | | | |  | |  | |  | |
|  |  |
|  | |  | |  | |  | |  | |  |  |  | |  | |  | |  | |  | |  | |  | |
|
|  | |  | |  | |  | | Yolanda **García Acosta**  *(la señora García)* | | | | | |  | |  | |  | |  | |  | |  | |
|

**Spanish names** are the traditional way of identifying, and the official way of registering a person in [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"). They are composed of a [given name](/wiki/Given_name "Given name") (simple or composite)[[a]](#cite_note-composite-2) and two [surnames](/wiki/Surname "Surname") (the first surname of each parent). Traditionally, the first surname is the father's first surname, and the second is the mother's first surname. Since 1999, the order of the surnames of the children in a family in Spain is decided when registering the first child, but the traditional order is nearly universally chosen (99.53% of the time).[[2]](#cite_note-3)[[b]](#cite_note-6) Women generally do not change their name with marriage.

The practice is to use one given name and the first surname generally (e.g. "[Penélope Cruz](/wiki/Pen%C3%A9lope_Cruz "Penélope Cruz")" for Penélope Cruz Sánchez); the complete name is reserved for legal, formal and documentary matters. Both surnames are sometimes systematically used when the first surname is very common (e.g., [Federico García Lorca](/wiki/Federico_Garc%C3%ADa_Lorca "Federico García Lorca"), [Pablo Ruiz Picasso](/wiki/Pablo_Picasso "Pablo Picasso") or [José Luis Rodríguez Zapatero](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Luis_Rodr%C3%ADguez_Zapatero "José Luis Rodríguez Zapatero")) to get a more distinguishable name.[[5]](#cite_note-7) In these cases, it is even common to use only the second surname, as in "Lorca", "Picasso" or "Zapatero". This does not affect alphabetization: "Federico García Lorca", the Spanish poet, should be alphabetized in an index under "García Lorca", not "Lorca".

Spanish naming customs were extended to countries under Spanish rule, influencing [naming customs of Hispanic America](/wiki/Naming_customs_of_Hispanic_America "Naming customs of Hispanic America") and [Philippines](/wiki/Filipino_name "Filipino name") to different extent.

## Basic structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_naming_customs&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Basic structure")]

Currently in Spain, people bear a single or composite [given name](/wiki/Given_name "Given name") (**nombre** in Spanish) and two [surnames](/wiki/Surnames "Surnames") (**apellidos** in Spanish).

A composite given name is composed of two (or more) single names; for example, *Juan Pablo* is considered not to be a first and a second forename, but a single composite forename.[[6]](#cite_note-8)

The two surnames refer to each of the parental families. Traditionally, a person's first surname is the father's first surname (**apellido paterno**), while their second surname is the mother's first surname (**apellido materno**). For example, if a man named *Eduardo Fernández Garrido* marries a woman named *María Dolores Martínez Ruiz* (note that women do not change their name with marriage) and they have a child named *José*, there are several legal options, but their child would most usually be known as *José Fernández Martínez*.

Spanish [gender equality](/wiki/Gender_equality "Gender equality") law has allowed surname transposition since 1999,[[7]](#cite_note-Orden_de_apellidos-9) subject to the condition that every sibling must bear the same surname order recorded in the **Registro Civil** ([civil registry](/wiki/Civil_registry "Civil registry")), but there have been legal exceptions. Since 2013, if the parents of a child were unable to agree on the order of surnames, an official would decide which is to come first,[[8]](#cite_note-10)[[9]](#cite_note-11)[[10]](#cite_note-12) with the paternal name being the default option. The only requirement is that every son and daughter must have the same order of the surnames, so they cannot change it separately. Since June 2017, adopting the paternal name first is no longer the standard method, and parents are required to sign an agreement wherein the name order is expressed explicitly.[[11]](#cite_note-13)[[12]](#cite_note-14)[[13]](#cite_note-elmundo2-15) The law also grants a person the option, upon reaching adulthood, of reversing the order of their surnames. However, this legislation only applies to Spanish citizens; people of other nationalities are issued the surname indicated by the laws of their original country.[[13]](#cite_note-elmundo2-15)

Each of these two surnames can also be composite in itself, with the parts usually linked by:

* the conjunction *y* or *e* (and),
* the preposition *de* (of), or
* a hyphen.

For example, a person's name might be *Juan Pablo Fernández de Calderón García-Iglesias*, consisting of a forename (*Juan Pablo*), a paternal surname (*Fernández de Calderón*), and a maternal surname (*García-Iglesias*).

### Forms of address

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_naming_customs&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Forms of address")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Spanish_naming_customs "Special:EditPage/Spanish naming customs") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners") in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(May 2023)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

A man named *José Antonio Gómez Iglesias* would normally be addressed as either *señor Gómez* or *señor Gómez Iglesias* instead of *señor Iglesias*, because *Gómez* is his first surname. Furthermore, Mr. Gómez might be informally addressed as

1. *José Antonio*
2. *José*
3. *Pepe* (nickname for José)
4. *Antonio*
5. *Toño* (nickname for Antonio)
6. *Joselito, Josito, Joselillo, Josico* or *Joselín* (diminutives of José)
7. *Antoñito, Toñín*, *Toñito, Ñoño* or *Nono* (diminutives of Antonio)
8. *Joseán* ([apocopation](/wiki/Apocope "Apocope")).

Very formally, he could be addressed with an honorific such as *[don](/wiki/Don_(honorific) "Don (honorific)") José Antonio* or *don José*.

It is not unusual, when the first surname is very common, like *García* in the example above, for a person to be referred to formally using both family names, or casually by their second surname only. For example, [José Luis Rodríguez Zapatero](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Luis_Rodr%C3%ADguez_Zapatero "José Luis Rodríguez Zapatero") (elected President of the Spanish Government in the 2004 and 2008 general elections) is often called simply Zapatero, the name he inherited from his mother's family since Rodríguez is a common surname and may be ambiguous. The same occurs with another former Spanish Socialist leader, [Alfredo Pérez Rubalcaba](/wiki/Alfredo_P%C3%A9rez_Rubalcaba "Alfredo Pérez Rubalcaba"), with the poet and dramatist [Federico García Lorca](/wiki/Federico_Garc%C3%ADa_Lorca "Federico García Lorca"), and with the painter [Pablo Ruiz Picasso](/wiki/Pablo_Picasso "Pablo Picasso"). As these people's paternal surnames are very common, they are often referred to by their maternal surnames (Rubalcaba, Lorca, Picasso). It would nonetheless be a mistake to index Rodríguez Zapatero under Z or García Lorca under L. (Picasso, who spent most of his adult life in France, is normally indexed under "P".)

In an English-speaking environment, Spanish-named people sometimes [hyphenate](/wiki/Hyphen "Hyphen") their surnames to avoid having their first surname perceived as a middle name, and their second surname perceived as their only surname.[[14]](#cite_note-16)

In Spanish-speaking countries, hyphenated surnames arise when someone wants both the paternal and maternal surnames passed to future generations, and the next generation receives the two, hyphenated, as a single (paternal) surname. Occasionally the two are fused into a simple (unhyphenated) name, such as [Jovellanos](/wiki/Gaspar_Melchor_de_Jovellanos "Gaspar Melchor de Jovellanos") (from Jove and Llanos). Rarely, the two names are left unhyphenated, such as [López Portillo](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_L%C3%B3pez_Portillo "José López Portillo"), which may lead to confusion.

### Forenames

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_naming_customs&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Forenames")]

Parents choose their child's [given name](/wiki/Given_name "Given name"), which must be recorded in the **Registro Civil** (Civil Registry) to establish their legal identity.[[15]](#cite_note-17) With few restrictions, parents can now choose any name; common sources of names are the parents' taste, honouring a relative, the [General Roman Calendar](/wiki/General_Roman_Calendar "General Roman Calendar") *nomina* (nominal register), and traditional Spanish names. Legislation in [Spain under Franco](/wiki/Spain_under_Franco "Spain under Franco")'s dictatorship legally limited cultural naming customs to only [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") (Jesus, Mary, saints)[[16]](#cite_note-18) and typical Spanish names (Álvaro, Jimena, etc.). Although the first part of a composite forename generally reflects the sex of the child, the second personal name need not (e.g. [José María Aznar](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Mar%C3%ADa_Aznar "José María Aznar")). At present, the only naming limitation is the dignity of the child, who cannot be given an insulting name. Similar limitations applied against diminutive, familiar, and colloquial variants not recognized as names proper, and "those that lead to confusion regarding sex";[[17]](#cite_note-19)
however, current law[[18]](#cite_note-''Identidad_de_género''-20) allows registration of diminutive names.[[19]](#cite_note-Pepe-21)

#### María, José and Jesús in composite given names

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/5/52/Concentracion_apellidos_por_provincias_Espa%C3%B1a.png)](/wiki/File:Concentracion_apellidos_por_provincias_Espa%C3%B1a.png)

Spanish provincial surname concentrations: percentage of population born with the ten most-common surnames for each province (source: [Instituto Nacional de Estadística](/wiki/Instituto_Nacional_de_Estad%C3%ADstica_(Spain) "Instituto Nacional de Estadística (Spain)") 2006)

Girls are often named *María*,[[20]](#cite_note-22) honouring the [Virgin Mary](/wiki/Mary_(mother_of_Jesus) "Mary (mother of Jesus)"), by appending either a shrine, place, or religious-concept suffix-name to *María*. In daily life, such women omit the "Mary of the ..." nominal prefix, and use the suffix portion of their composite names as their public, rather than legal, [identity](/wiki/Personally_identifiable_information "Personally identifiable information"). Hence, women with [Marian](/wiki/Blessed_Virgin_Mary_(Roman_Catholic) "Blessed Virgin Mary (Roman Catholic)") names such as *María de los Ángeles* (María of the Angels), *María del Pilar* ([María of the Pillar](/wiki/Our_Lady_of_the_Pillar "Our Lady of the Pillar")), and *María de la Luz* (María of the Light), are normally addressed as *Ángeles* (Angels), *Pilar* (Pillar), and *Luz* (Light); however, each might be addressed as *María*. Nicknames such as *Maricarmen* for *María del Carmen*, *Marisol* for "María (de la) Soledad" ("Our Lady of Solitude", the Virgin Mary), *Dolores* or *Lola* for *María de los Dolores* ("Our Lady of Sorrows"), *Mercedes* or *Merche* for *María de las Mercedes* ("Our Lady of Mercy"), etc. are often used. Also, parents can simply name a girl *María*, or *Mari* without a suffix portion.

It is common for a boy's formal name to include *María*, preceded by a masculine name, e.g. [José María Aznar](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Mar%C3%ADa_Aznar "José María Aznar"), [Juan María Vicencio de Ripperdá](/wiki/Juan_Mar%C3%ADa_Vicencio_de_Ripperd%C3%A1 "Juan María Vicencio de Ripperdá") or [Antonio María Rouco Varela](/wiki/Antonio_Mar%C3%ADa_Rouco_Varela "Antonio María Rouco Varela"). Equivalently, a girl can be formally named *María José*, e.g. skier [María José Rienda](/wiki/Mar%C3%ADa_Jos%C3%A9_Rienda "María José Rienda"), and informally named *Marijose*, *Mariajo*, *Majo*, *Ajo*, *Marisé* or even *José* in honour of St. Joseph. *María* as a masculine name is often abbreviated in writing as *M.* (José M. Aznar), *Ma.* (José Ma. Aznar), or *M.ª* ([José M.ª Morelos](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Mar%C3%ADa_Morelos "José María Morelos")).[[21]](#cite_note-23) It is unusual for any names other than the religiously significant *María* and *José* to be used in this way except for the name *Jesús* that is also very common and can be used as *Jesús* or *Jesús María* for a boy and *María Jesús* for a girl, and can be abbreviated as *Sus*, *Chus* and other nicknames.

The **Registro Civil** (Civil Registry) officially records a child's identity as composed of a [forename](/wiki/Given_name "Given name") (simple or composite) and the two [surnames](/wiki/Family_name "Family name"); however, a child can be religiously [baptized](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism") with several forenames, e.g. [Felipe Juan Froilán de Todos los Santos](/wiki/Felipe_Juan_Froil%C3%A1n_de_Marichalar_y_Borb%C3%B3n "Felipe Juan Froilán de Marichalar y Borbón"). Until the 1960s, it was customary to baptize children with three forenames: the first was the main and the only one used by the child; if parents agreed, one of the other two was the name of the day's saint. Nowadays, baptizing with three or more forenames is usually a [royal](/wiki/Royal_family "Royal family") and [noble](/wiki/Nobility "Nobility") family practice.

### Marriage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_naming_customs&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Marriage")]

In Spain married people keep their original surnames (unlike in some near cultures in which they may adopt the spouse's family name as a [married name](/wiki/Maiden_and_married_names "Maiden and married names")). In some instances, such as high society meetings, the partner's surname can be added after the person's surnames using the preposition *de* (of). An example would be a *Leocadia Blanco Álvarez*, married to a *Pedro Pérez Montilla*, may be addressed as *Leocadia Blanco de Pérez* or as *Leocadia Blanco Álvarez de Pérez*. This format is not used in everyday settings and has no legal value.[[22]](#cite_note-24)

Similarly, a widow may be identified using "viuda de" ("widow of" in Spanish) or its abbreviation "vda." for, as in *Leocadia Blanco vda. de Pérez*.

### Generational transmission

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_naming_customs&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Generational transmission")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Spanish_surnames_by_province_of_residence.png/500px-Spanish_surnames_by_province_of_residence.png)](/wiki/File:Spanish_surnames_by_province_of_residence.png)

Surname distribution: the most common surnames in Spain, by [province](/wiki/Provinces_of_Spain "Provinces of Spain") of residence

In the generational transmission of surnames, the paternal surname's precedence eventually eliminates the maternal surnames from the family [lineage](/wiki/Lineal_descendant "Lineal descendant"). Contemporary law (1999) allows the maternal surname to be given precedence, but most people observe the traditional paternal–maternal surname order. Therefore, the daughter and son of *Ángela López Sáenz* and *Tomás Portillo Blanco* are usually called *Laura Portillo López* and *Pedro Portillo López* but could also be called *Laura López Portillo* and *Pedro López Portillo*. The two surnames of all siblings must be in the same order when recorded in the **Registro Civil**. Spanish naming customs include the [orthographic](/wiki/Orthography "Orthography") option of conjoining the surnames with the [conjunction](/wiki/Grammatical_conjunction "Grammatical conjunction") particle *[y](#The_particle_"y"_(and))*, or *e* before a name starting with 'I', 'Hi' or 'Y', (both meaning "and") (e.g., *[José Ortega y Gasset](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Ortega_y_Gasset "José Ortega y Gasset")*, *Tomás Portillo y Blanco*, or *[Eduardo Dato e Iradier](/wiki/Eduardo_Dato_e_Iradier "Eduardo Dato e Iradier")*), following an antiquated [aristocratic](/wiki/Aristocracy_(class) "Aristocracy (class)") usage.

Patrilineal surname transmission was not always the [norm](/wiki/Norm_(sociology) "Norm (sociology)") in Spanish-speaking societies. Prior to the mid-eighteenth century,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] when the current paternal-maternal surname combination norm was adopted, Hispanophone societies often practised matrilineal surname transmission, giving children the maternal surname and occasionally giving children a grandparent's surname (borne by neither parent) for prestige – being perceived as [gentry](/wiki/Gentry "Gentry") – and profit, flattering the [matriarch](/wiki/Matriarchy "Matriarchy") or the [patriarch](/wiki/Patriarchy "Patriarchy") in hope of [inheriting](/wiki/Inheritance "Inheritance") land. A more recent example can be found in the name of *[Francisco de Asís Franco y Martínez-Bordiú](/wiki/Francis_Franco "Francis Franco")* (born 1954), who took first the name of his mother, [Carmen Franco](/wiki/Carmen_Franco "Carmen Franco"), rather than that of his father, [Cristóbal Martínez-Bordiú, 10th Marquis of Villaverde](/wiki/Crist%C3%B3bal_Mart%C3%ADnez-Bordi%C3%BA,_10th_Marquis_of_Villaverde "Cristóbal Martínez-Bordiú, 10th Marquis of Villaverde"), in order to perpetuate the family name of his maternal grandfather, the *[Caudillo](/wiki/Caudillo "Caudillo")* [Francisco Franco](/wiki/Francisco_Franco "Francisco Franco").[[23]](#cite_note-mundo-25)

Not every surname is a single word; such conjoining usage is common with doubled surnames (maternal-paternal), ancestral composite surnames [bequeathed](/wiki/Inheritance "Inheritance") to the following generations – especially when the paternal surname is socially undistinguished. *[José María Álvarez del Manzano y López del Hierro](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Mar%C3%ADa_%C3%81lvarez_del_Manzano "José María Álvarez del Manzano")* is an example, his name comprising the composite single name *José María* and two composite surnames, *Álvarez del Manzano* and *López del Hierro*. Other examples derive from church place-names such as San José. When a person bears doubled surnames, the means of disambiguation is to insert *y* between the paternal and maternal surnames.

In case of [illegitimacy](/wiki/Legitimacy_(family_law) "Legitimacy (family law)") – when the child's father either is unknown or refuses to recognize his child legally – the child bears both of the mother's surnames, which may be interchanged.[[24]](#cite_note-Single-parent-26)

Occasionally, a person with a common paternal surname and an uncommon maternal surname becomes widely known by the maternal surname. Some examples include the artist [Pablo Ruiz Picasso](/wiki/Pablo_Picasso "Pablo Picasso"), the poet [Federico García Lorca](/wiki/Federico_Garc%C3%ADa_Lorca "Federico García Lorca"), and the politician [José Luis Rodríguez Zapatero](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Luis_Rodr%C3%ADguez_Zapatero "José Luis Rodríguez Zapatero"). With a similar effect, the foreign paternal surname of the Uruguayan writer [Eduardo Hughes Galeano](/wiki/Eduardo_Galeano "Eduardo Galeano") (his father was British) is usually omitted. (As a boy, however, he occasionally signed his name as *Eduardo Gius*, using a Hispanicized approximation of the English pronunciation of "Hughes".) Such use of the second last name by itself is colloquial, however, and may not be applied in legal contexts.

Also rarely, a person may become widely known by both surnames, with an example being a tennis player [Arantxa Sánchez Vicario](/wiki/Arantxa_S%C3%A1nchez_Vicario "Arantxa Sánchez Vicario") – whereas her older brothers [Emilio](/wiki/Emilio_S%C3%A1nchez "Emilio Sánchez") and [Javier](/wiki/Javier_S%C3%A1nchez_(tennis) "Javier Sánchez (tennis)"), also professional tennis players, are mainly known only by the paternal surname of Sánchez in everyday life, although they would *formally* be addressed as *Sánchez Vicario*.

### Navarrese and Álavan surnames

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_naming_customs&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Navarrese and Álavan surnames")]

Where [Basque](/wiki/Basque_culture "Basque culture") and Romance cultures have linguistically long coexisted, the surnames denote the father's name and the (family) [house](/wiki/Family "Family") or town/village. Thus the Romance [patronymic](/wiki/Patronym "Patronym") and the place-name are conjoined with the prepositional particle *de* ("from"+"provenance"). For example, in the name *[José Ignacio López de Arriortúa](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Ignacio_L%C3%B3pez_de_Arriort%C3%BAa "José Ignacio López de Arriortúa")*, the composite surname *López de Arriortúa* is a single surname, despite *Arriortúa* being the original family name. This can lead to confusion because the Spanish *López* and the Basque *Arriortúa* are discrete surnames in Spanish and Basque respectively. This pattern was also in use in other Basque districts, but was phased out in most of the Basque-speaking areas and only remained in place across lands of heavy Romance influence, i.e. some central areas of [Navarre](/wiki/Navarre "Navarre") and most of [Álava](/wiki/%C3%81lava "Álava"). To a lesser extent, this pattern has been also present in Castile, where [Basque](/wiki/Basque_language "Basque language")-[Castilian](/wiki/Castilian_Spanish "Castilian Spanish") bilingualism was common in northern and eastern areas up to the 13th century.

A notable example of this system was *Joaquina Sánchez de Samaniego y Fernández de Tejada*, with both paternal and maternal surnames coming from this system, joined with an *y* ("and").

## Nominal conjunctions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_naming_customs&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Nominal conjunctions")]

### The particle "de" (of)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_naming_customs&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: The particle \"de\" (of)")]

See also: [Spanish nobility](/wiki/Spanish_nobility "Spanish nobility") and [Naming customs of Hispanic America § The particle "de" (of)](/wiki/Naming_customs_of_Hispanic_America#The_particle_"de"_(of) "Naming customs of Hispanic America")

In Spanish, the [preposition](/wiki/Spanish_prepositions "Spanish prepositions") particle *de* ("of") is used as a [conjunction](/wiki/Spanish_grammar "Spanish grammar") in two different surname styles, and also used in a kind of placeholder role to disambiguate surnames that might be mistaken as additional forenames. The first style is in [patronymic](/wiki/Patronymy "Patronymy") and [toponymic surname](/wiki/Toponymic_surname "Toponymic surname") formulæ,[[25]](#cite_note-27) e.g. *[Gonzalo Fernández de Córdoba](/wiki/Gonzalo_Fern%C3%A1ndez_de_C%C3%B3rdoba "Gonzalo Fernández de Córdoba")*, *[Pedro López de Ayala](/wiki/Pedro_L%C3%B3pez_de_Ayala "Pedro López de Ayala")*, and *[Vasco Núñez de Balboa](/wiki/Vasco_N%C3%BA%C3%B1ez_de_Balboa "Vasco Núñez de Balboa")*, as in many *[conquistador](/wiki/Conquistador "Conquistador")* names.[[26]](#cite_note-28)

In names of persons, the [prepositional](/wiki/Preposition "Preposition") particle *de* is written in lower-case when the forename has been included, e.g. *José Manuel de la Rúa* ("of the street") and *Cunegunda de la Torre* ("of the tower"); when the forename has been omitted, the *de* is capitalized, e.g. *doctor De la Rúa* and *señora De la Torre*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Without a patronymic
:   [Juan Carlos de Borbón](/wiki/Juan_Carlos_I_of_Spain "Juan Carlos I of Spain"). Unlike in French names such as d'Alembert, in [Spanish orthography](/wiki/Spanish_orthography "Spanish orthography") "de" is written out in full (not replaced by a contraction) when the surname begins with a vowel. The exception is *de el* ("of the"), which becomes *del*, e.g. *Carlos Arturo del Monte* (Charles Arthur of the Mountain).

The patronymic exception
:   The current (1958) Spanish name law, **Artículo 195 del Reglamento del Registro Civil** (Article 195 of the Civil Registry Regulations) does not allow a person to prefix *de* to their surname, except as the clarifying addition of *de* to a surname (*apellido*) that might be misunderstood as a forename (**nombre**);[[27]](#cite_note-29) thus, a child would be registered as *Pedro de Miguel Jiménez* to avoid the surname *Miguel* being mistaken as the second part of a composite name, as *Pedro Miguel*.

Bearing the *de* particle does not necessarily denote a noble family; especially in names from eastern [Castile](/wiki/Crown_of_Castile "Crown of Castile"), [Alava](/wiki/Alava "Alava"), and western Navarre, the *de* usually meant simply "from", and was applied to the place-name (town or village) from which the person and his or her ancestors originated. This differs from another practice established in the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries, in which *de* could be applied to one's own name as a way of denoting the bearer's noble heritage, to avoid the misperception that he or she was either a Jew or a Moor. In that time, many people, regardless of their true origins, used the particle, e.g. *[Miguel de Cervantes](/wiki/Miguel_de_Cervantes "Miguel de Cervantes")*, *[Lope de Vega](/wiki/Lope_de_Vega "Lope de Vega")*, etc.; moreover, following that fashion, a high noble such as *Francisco Sandoval Rojas* called himself *Francisco de Sandoval y Rojas*. During the eighteenth century, the Spanish nobility fully embraced the French custom of using *de* as a nobility identifier; however, since many commoners also bore the same particle, the use of *de* became unclear. Thus, nobility was emphasised with the surname's lineage.

### The particle "y" (and)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_naming_customs&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: The particle \"y\" (and)")]

In the sixteenth century,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] the Spanish adopted the copulative [conjunction](/wiki/Grammatical_conjunction "Grammatical conjunction") *y* ("and") to distinguish a person's surnames; thus the Andalusian Baroque writer [Luis de Góngora y Argote](/wiki/Luis_de_G%C3%B3ngora "Luis de Góngora") (1561–1627), the Aragonese painter [Francisco José de Goya y Lucientes](/wiki/Francisco_Goya "Francisco Goya") (1746–1828), the Andalusian artist [Pablo Diego Ruiz y Picasso](/wiki/Picasso "Picasso") (1881–1973), and the Madrilenian liberal philosopher [José Ortega y Gasset](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Ortega_y_Gasset "José Ortega y Gasset") (1883–1955). In Hispanic America, this spelling convention was common among clergymen (e.g. Salvadoran Bishop [Óscar Arnulfo Romero y Galdámez](/wiki/%C3%93scar_Arnulfo_Romero_y_Gald%C3%A1mez "Óscar Arnulfo Romero y Galdámez")), and sanctioned by the **Ley de Registro Civil** (Civil Registry Law) of 1870, which required birth certificates to indicate the paternal and maternal surnames conjoined with *y* – thus, *Felipe González y Márquez* and *José María Aznar y López* are the respective legal names of the Spanish politicians [Felipe González Márquez](/wiki/Felipe_Gonz%C3%A1lez "Felipe González") and [José María Aznar López](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Mar%C3%ADa_Aznar "José María Aznar"); however, unlike in [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language"), this usage is infrequent in Spanish. In the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), *y* and its associated usages are retained only in formal state documents such as police records, but is otherwise dropped in favour of a more American-influenced naming order.

The conjunction *y* avoids denominational confusion when the paternal surname might appear to be a (first) name: without it, the physiologist [Santiago Ramón y Cajal](/wiki/Santiago_Ram%C3%B3n_y_Cajal "Santiago Ramón y Cajal") might appear to be named *Santiago Ramón* (composite) and surnamed *Cajal*, likewise the jurist *Francisco Tomás y Valiente*, and the cleric *Vicente Enrique y Tarancón*. Without the conjunction, the [footballer](/wiki/Football_(soccer) "Football (soccer)") [Rafael Martín Vázquez](/wiki/Rafael_Mart%C3%ADn_V%C3%A1zquez "Rafael Martín Vázquez"), when referred to by his surnames *Martín Vázquez* mistakenly appears to be forenamed *Martín* rather than *Rafael*, whilst, to his annoyance, the linguist [Fernando Lázaro Carreter](/wiki/Fernando_L%C3%A1zaro_Carreter "Fernando Lázaro Carreter") occasionally was addressed as *Don Lázaro*, rather than as *Don Fernando* (Lázaro can be either forename or surname).

When the conjunction *y* is used and the maternal surname begins with an *i* vowel sound — whether written with the vowel *I* (*Ibarra*), the vowel *Y* (*Ybarra* archaic spelling), or the combination *Hi* + consonant (*[Higueras](/wiki/Higueras_(surname) "Higueras (surname)")*) — Spanish [euphony](/wiki/Euphony "Euphony") substitutes *e* in place of the word *y*; thus the example of the Spanish statesman *[Eduardo Dato e Iradier](/wiki/Eduardo_Dato_e_Iradier "Eduardo Dato e Iradier")* (1856–1921).

## Denotations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_naming_customs&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Denotations")]

To communicate a person's [social identity](/wiki/Identity_(social_science) "Identity (social science)"), Spanish naming customs provide [orthographic](/wiki/Orthography "Orthography") means, such as suffix-letter abbreviations, [surname](/wiki/Surname "Surname") spellings, and place names, which denote and connote the person's place in [society](/wiki/Society "Society").

### Identity and descent

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_naming_customs&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Identity and descent")]

**p.** (father of): A man named like his son may add the lower-case suffix **p.** (denoting *padre*, father) to his surname. An example of this is *José Luis Lorena, p.*, to distinguish him from his son *José Luis Lorena*; the English analogue is "Sr." (*senior*).

**h.** (son of): A man named like his father may append the lower-case suffix **h.** (denoting *hijo*, son) to his surname, thus distinguishing himself, *Juan Gómez Marcos, h.*, from his father, *Juan Gómez Marcos*; the English analogue is "Jr." (*junior*).

#### The suffix -ez

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_naming_customs&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: The suffix -ez")]

Following the [Visigothic](/wiki/Visigoth "Visigoth") invasion of the Iberian peninsula, the local population adopted to a large extent a [patronymic](/wiki/Patronymic "Patronymic") naming system: the suffix *-icī* (a [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") [genitive](/wiki/Genitive "Genitive") meaning *son of*) would be attached to the father's forename to create a patronymic for the son.[[28]](#cite_note-30)[[29]](#cite_note-31) This suffix gradually evolved into different local forms, depending on the language. For example, the son of [Fernando](/wiki/Ferdinand "Ferdinand") would be called:

* [Basque](/wiki/Basque_language "Basque language"): Fernanditz[[30]](#cite_note-Fernanditz-32)
* [Castillan](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): Fernández
* [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language"): Ferrandis
* [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language") and [Galician](/wiki/Galician_language "Galician language"): Fernandes

This system was most common in, but not limited to, the central region of [Castile](/wiki/Castile_(historical_region) "Castile (historical region)"). Bare surnames, i.e. the father's name without the suffix -itz/-ez/-is/-es, can also be found, and are especially common in [Catalonia](/wiki/Catalonia "Catalonia"). This said, mass migration in the 20th century has led to a certain levelling of such regional differences.

In [Catalan speaking areas](/wiki/Pa%C3%AFsos_Catalans "Països Catalans"), the suffixed surname *Ferrandis* is most common in the South (the [Valencian Country](/wiki/Valencian_Country "Valencian Country")) while in the North ([Catalonia](/wiki/Catalonia "Catalonia")) the bare surname *Ferran* is more common. Furthermore, language contact led to the creation of multiple hybrid forms, as evidenced by the multiple Catalano-Castillan surnames, found especially in the Valencian Country: *Fernàndez*, *Fernandis*, *Fernàndiz*, *Ferrandez*, *Ferràniz*, *Ferranis*, etc.

Not every surname that resembles this pattern is patronymic. Due to the letters *z* and *s* being pronounced alike in American dialects of Spanish, many non-patronymic surnames with an *-es* have come to be written with an *-ez*. In American [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"), the *-ez* spellings of *Chávez* ([Hugo Chávez](/wiki/Hugo_Ch%C3%A1vez "Hugo Chávez")), *Cortez* ([Alberto Cortez](/wiki/Alberto_Cortez "Alberto Cortez")) and *Valdez* ([Nelson Valdez](/wiki/Nelson_Valdez "Nelson Valdez")) are not patronymic surnames, but simply variant spellings of the [Iberian](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula") Spanish spelling with *-es*, as in the names of [Manuel Chaves](/wiki/Manuel_Chaves_Gonz%C3%A1lez "Manuel Chaves González"), [Hernán Cortés](/wiki/Hern%C3%A1n_Cort%C3%A9s "Hernán Cortés") and [Víctor Valdés](/wiki/V%C3%ADctor_Vald%C3%A9s "Víctor Valdés"). For more on the *-z* surnames in Spanish see [Influences on the Spanish language](/wiki/Influences_on_the_Spanish_language "Influences on the Spanish language").

### Foundlings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_naming_customs&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Foundlings")]

Anonymous [abandoned children](/wiki/Child_abandonment "Child abandonment") were a problem for civil registrars to name. Some such children were named after the town where they were found ([toponymic surname](/wiki/Toponymic_surname "Toponymic surname")). Because most were reared in church orphanages, some were also given the surnames *Iglesia* or *Iglesias* (church[es]) and *Cruz* (cross). *Blanco* (with the meaning "blank", rather than "white") was another option. A toponymic first surname might have been followed by *Iglesia(s)* or *Cruz* as a second surname.

Nameless children were sometimes given the surname *Expósito*/*Expósita* (from [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") *exposĭtus*, "exposed", meaning "abandoned child"), which marked them, and their descendants,[[31]](#cite_note-33) as of a low [caste](/wiki/Caste "Caste") or [social class](/wiki/Social_class "Social class"). Due to this, in 1921 Spanish law started to allow holders of the surname *Expósito* to legally change their surname.[[32]](#cite_note-34) In the [Catalan language](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language"), the surname *Deulofeu* ("made by God") was often given out to these children, which is similar to *De Dios* ("from God") in Castilian.

Furthermore, in [Aragón](/wiki/Aragon "Aragon") abandoned children would receive the surname *Gracia* ("grace") or *de Gracia,* because they were thought to survive by the [grace](/wiki/Grace_(Christianity) "Grace (Christianity)") of God.

### Foreign citizens

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_naming_customs&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Foreign citizens")]

In Spain, foreign immigrants retain use of their cultural naming customs,[[33]](#cite_note-35) but upon becoming Spanish [citizens](/wiki/Citizenship "Citizenship"), they are legally obliged to assume Spanish-style names (one forename and two surnames).[[34]](#cite_note-36) If the [naturalized](/wiki/Nationality "Nationality") citizen is from a one-surname culture, either their current surname is doubled or their mother's [maiden name](/wiki/Maiden_and_married_names "Maiden and married names") is adopted as the second surname. For example, a Briton with the name "Sarah Jane Smith" could become either "Sarah Jane Smith Smith" or "Sarah Jane Smith Jones" upon acquiring Spanish citizenship. Formally, Spanish naming customs would also mean that the [forename](/wiki/Given_name "Given name") "Sarah" and [middle name](/wiki/Middle_name "Middle name") "Jane" would be treated as a compound forename: "Sarah Jane".

### Flamenco artists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_naming_customs&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Flamenco artists")]

Historically, [flamenco](/wiki/Flamenco "Flamenco") artists seldom used their proper names. According to the flamenco guitarist [Juan Serrano](/wiki/Juan_Serrano_(Flamenco) "Juan Serrano (Flamenco)"), this was because flamenco was considered disreputable and they did not want to embarrass their families:

> We have to start with the history of the gypsies in Spain. They gained a bad reputation because of the minor crimes they had to commit to survive. They did not have any kind of jobs, they had to do something to live, and of course this created hostility. And Flamenco was the music of the Gypsies, so many high society people did not accept it – they said Flamenco was in the hands of criminals, bandits, et cetera. And the girls, that maybe liked dancing or singing, their parents said, "Oh no, you want to be a prostitute!".

— Juan Serrano, interview in *Guitar International*, Nov 1987

This tradition of not using one's proper name has persisted to the present day, even though flamenco is now legitimate. Sometimes the artistic name consists of the home town appended to the first name ([Manolo Sanlúcar](/wiki/Manolo_Sanl%C3%BAcar "Manolo Sanlúcar"), [Ramón de Algeciras](/wiki/Ram%C3%B3n_de_Algeciras "Ramón de Algeciras")); but many, perhaps most, such names are more eccentric: Pepe de la Matrona (because his mother was a midwife); [Perico del Lunar](/w/index.php?title=Perico_del_Lunar&action=edit&redlink=1 "Perico del Lunar (page does not exist)") (because he had a mole); [Tomatito](/wiki/Tomatito "Tomatito") (son of a father known as *Tomate* (tomato) because of his red face); [Sabicas](/wiki/Sabicas "Sabicas") (because of his childhood passion for green beans, from *niño de las habicas*); [Paco de Lucía](/wiki/Paco_de_Luc%C3%ADa "Paco de Lucía"), born Francisco ("[Paco](/wiki/Paco "Paco")") Gustavo Sánchez Gomes, was known from infancy after his Portuguese mother, Lucía Gomes (de Lucía = [son] of Lucía). And many more. When referring to these artists by their assumed names, it makes no sense to shorten the name to its qualifier, such as "Lucía" or "de Lucía"; Paco, or perhaps "el de Lucía", are the only options.

### Spanish hypocoristics and nicknames

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_naming_customs&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Spanish hypocoristics and nicknames")]

Many Spanish names can be shortened into [hypocoristic](/wiki/Hypocoristic "Hypocoristic"), affectionate "[child-talk](/wiki/Baby_talk "Baby talk")" forms using a [diminutive](/wiki/Diminutive "Diminutive") suffix, especially **-ito** and **-cito** (masculine) and **-ita** and **-cita** (feminine). Sometimes longer than the person's name, a [nickname](/wiki/Nickname "Nickname") is usually derived via [linguistic](/wiki/Linguistics "Linguistics") rules.[[35]](#cite_note-37) However, in contrast to English use, hypocoristic names in Spanish are only used to address a person in a very familiar environment – the only exception being when the hypocoristic is an artistic name (e.g. [Nacho Duato](/wiki/Nacho_Duato "Nacho Duato") born Juan Ignacio Duato). The common English practice of using a nickname in the press or media, or even on business cards (such as [Bill Gates](/wiki/Bill_Gates "Bill Gates") instead of William Gates), is not accepted in Spanish, being considered excessively colloquial. ## Spain's other languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_naming_customs&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Spain's other languages")]

The official recognition of Spain's [other written languages](/wiki/Regional_language "Regional language") – [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language"), [Basque](/wiki/Basque_language "Basque language"), and [Galician](/wiki/Galician_language "Galician language") – legally allowed the autonomous communities to re-establish their vernacular [social identity](/wiki/Identity_(social_science) "Identity (social science)"), including the legal use of personal names in the local languages and written traditions; these had been banned since 1938.[[36]](#cite_note-38) This has sometimes been accomplished by re-spelling names to change Castilian Spanish forms into their original languages.

### Basque names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_naming_customs&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Basque names")]

See also: [Basque language](/wiki/Basque_language "Basque language") and [Basque surnames](/wiki/Basque_surnames "Basque surnames")

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Spanish_naming_customs "Special:EditPage/Spanish naming customs") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(July 2010)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

The Basque-speaking territories (the [Basque Autonomous Community](/wiki/Basque_Country_(autonomous_community) "Basque Country (autonomous community)") and [Navarre](/wiki/Navarre "Navarre")) follow Spanish naming customs (given names + two family names, the two family names being usually the father's and the mother's).

The given names are officially in one language or the other (Basque or Spanish), but often people use a translated or shortened version. A bilingual Basque-Spanish speaker will not necessarily bear a Basque name, and a monolingual Spanish speaker can use a Basque name or a Basque hypocoristic of an official Spanish name; e.g. a *Francisco* (official Spanish name) may be known as *Patxi* (Basque hypocoristic).

Some Basque-language names and surnames are foreign transliterations into the Basque tongue, e.g., *[Ander](/wiki/Ander "Ander")* (English: "[Andrew](/wiki/Andrew "Andrew")"; Spanish: *Andrés*), *Mikel* (English: "Michael"; Spanish: *[Miguel](/wiki/Miguel_(disambiguation) "Miguel (disambiguation)")*), or *Ane* (English: "[Anne](/wiki/Anne "Anne")"; Spanish: *Ana*). In some cases, the name's [original-language denotation is translated](/wiki/Calque "Calque") to Basque, e.g., *Zutoia* and *Zedarri* denote the Spanish *[Pilar](/wiki/Our_Lady_of_the_Pillar "Our Lady of the Pillar")* (English: "Pillar"). Moreover, some originally Basque names, such as *Xabier* and *Eneko* (English "[Xavier](/wiki/Xavier_(given_name) "Xavier (given name)")" and "[Inigo](/wiki/Inigo "Inigo")"), have been transliterated into Spanish (*[Javier](/wiki/Javier_(name) "Javier (name)")* and *[Íñigo](/wiki/%C3%8D%C3%B1igo "Íñigo")*).

Recently, Basque names without a direct equivalent in other languages have become popular, e.g. *[Aitor](/wiki/Aitor "Aitor")* (a legendary patriarch), *Hodei* ("cloud"), *[Iker](/wiki/Iker "Iker")* ("to investigate"), and *[Amaia](/wiki/Amaia "Amaia")* ("the end"). Some Basque names without a definable meaning in Spanish are unique to the Basque language, for instance, *Eneko*, *[Garikoitz](/wiki/Garikoitz "Garikoitz")*, *[Urtzi](/wiki/Urtzi "Urtzi")*. After Franco's death and the restoration of democracy in Spain, many Basque adults changed their Spanish names to their Basque equivalents, e.g. from *Miguel* to *Mikel*.

A source for modern Basque names is [Sabino Arana](/wiki/Sabino_Arana "Sabino Arana")'s *[Deun-Ixendegi Euzkotarra](/w/index.php?title=Deun-Ixendegi_Euzkotarra&action=edit&redlink=1 "Deun-Ixendegi Euzkotarra (page does not exist)")* ("Basque saint-name collection", published in 1910). Instead of the traditional Basque adaptations of Romance names, he proposed others he made up, and which in his opinion were truer to the originals and adapted better to Basque phonology. For example, [his brother Luis](/wiki/Luis_Arana_(politician) "Luis Arana (politician)") became *Koldobika*, from Frankish *Hlodwig*. The traditional names *Peru* (from Spanish "[Pedro](/wiki/Pedro "Pedro")"), *Pello* or *Piarres* (from French "[Pierre](/wiki/Pierre "Pierre")"), all meaning "[Peter](/wiki/Peter_(name) "Peter (name)")", became *Kepa* from Aramaic כיפא (Kepha). He believed that the suffix *-[n]e* was inherently feminine, and new names like *Nekane* ("pain"+*ne*, "[Dolores](/wiki/Dolores_(given_name) "Dolores (given name)")") or *Garbiñe* ("clean"+*ne*, "[Immaculate [Conception]](/wiki/Immaculate_Conception "Immaculate Conception")") are frequent among Basque females.

[Basque surnames](/wiki/Basque_surname "Basque surname") usually denote the paternal house (in its literal sense of a dwelling place) of the bearer; e.g. *[Etxebarria](/wiki/Etxebarria "Etxebarria")* – "the new house", from *etxe* (house) + *barri* (new) + *a* (the), denotes "related to a farmhouse of that name"; in the same way, *Garaikoetxea* – "the house in the heights", *garai* ("height") + *etxe* ("house") + *a* (the). Sometimes, surnames denote not the house itself but a characteristic of the place, e.g. *Saratxaga* – "willow-place", from *saratze* ("willow") + *-aga* ("place of"); *Loyola*, from *loi* ("mud") + *ola* ("iron smithery"); *[Arriortua](/w/index.php?title=Arriortua&action=edit&redlink=1 "Arriortua (page does not exist)")* – "stone orchard", from *harri* ("stone") + *ortua* ("orchard"). Before the 20th century all Basque men were considered [nobles](/wiki/Hidalgo_(Spanish_nobility) "Hidalgo (Spanish nobility)") (indeed, some Basque surnames, e.g. *Irujo* or *Medoza*, were related to some of the oldest Spanish noble families), and many of them used their status to emigrate with privileges to other regions of the Spanish Empire, especially the Americas; thus some Basque surnames became common in the Spanish-American world, e.g. *Mendoza* – "cold mountain", from *mendi* ("mountain") + *hotza* ("cold"); *[Salazar](/wiki/Salazar_(surname) "Salazar (surname)")* – "old hall", from *sala* ("hall") + *zahar* ("old"). Until 1978, Spanish was the single official language of the Spanish civil registries, and Basque surnames had to be registered according to the Spanish phonetical rules (for example, the Spanish "ch" sound merges the Basque "ts", "tx", and "tz", and someone whose surname in [Standard Basque](/wiki/Standard_Basque "Standard Basque") would be "Krutxaga" would have to write it as "Cruchaga", the letter "k" also not being used in Spanish). Although the restoration of democracy ended this policy and allowed surnames to be officially changed into their Basque orthography, surnames of Basque origin now often have more than one spelling, even within the same family: a father born before 1978 would be surnamed "Echepare" and his children, "Etxepare". This policy even changed the usual pronunciation of some Basque surnames. For instance, in Basque, the letter "z" maintained a sibilant "s"-like sound, while Spanish changed it; thus, a surname such as *Zabala* in Basque is properly read similar to "sabala" (Basque pronunciation: [[s̻abala]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Basque "Help:IPA/Basque")), but in Spanish, where the "z" denotes a "th" sound ([[θ](/wiki/Voiceless_dental_fricative "Voiceless dental fricative")]), it would be read as "Tha-bala" (Spanish pronunciation: [[θaˈβala]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Spanish "Help:IPA/Spanish")). However, since the letter "z" exists in Spanish, the registries did not force the Zabalas to transliterate their surname.

In the Basque provinces of [Biscay](/wiki/Biscay "Biscay") and [Gipuzkoa](/wiki/Gipuzkoa "Gipuzkoa"), it was uncommon to take a surname from the place (town or village) where one resided, unless one was a foundling; in general, people bearing surnames such as *Bilbao* (after the Basque city of [Bilbao](/wiki/Bilbao "Bilbao")) are descendants of foundlings. However, in the Basque province of [Alava](/wiki/Alava "Alava") and, to a lesser extent, in [Navarre](/wiki/Navarre "Navarre"), it was common to add one's birth village to the surname using the Spanish particle *de* to denote a *toponymic*, particularly when the surname was a common one; for instance, someone whose surname was *Lopez* and whose family was originally from the valley of *Ayala* could employ *Lopez de Ayala* as a surname. This latter practice is also common in [Castile](/wiki/Castile_(historical_region) "Castile (historical region)").

Basque compound surnames are relatively common, and were created by combining two discrete surnames, e.g. *Elorduizapaterietxe* – *Elordui* + *Zapaterietxe*, a practice denoting family allegiances or the equal importance of both families. Since compound surnames could themselves be used to create new compounds, this custom sometimes resulted in incredibly long surnames. For example, the longest surname recorded in Spain is the compound Basque name *Burionagonatotoricagageazcoechea*,[[37]](#cite_note-Albaigès-39) formed by *Buriona*+ *Gonatar* + *Totorika* + *Beazcoetxea*.

Basque nationalist leader Sabino Arana pioneered a naming custom of transposing the name-surname order to what he thought was the proper Basque language syntax order; e.g. the woman named *Miren Zabala* would be referred to as *Zabala'taŕ Miren* – the surname first, plus the *-tar* suffix denoting "from a place", and then the name. Thus, *Zabala'taŕ Miren* means "Miren, of the Zabala family". The change in the order is effected because in the Basque language, declined words (such as *Zabala'taŕ*) that apply to a noun are placed before the noun itself; another example of this would be his pen name, *Arana ta Goiri'taŕ Sabin*. This Basque naming custom was used in nationalist literature; in formal official documents, the Castilian naming order is observed.

### Catalan names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_naming_customs&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Catalan names")]

Further information: [Category:Catalan-language surnames](/wiki/Category:Catalan-language_surnames "Category:Catalan-language surnames")

The [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language")-speaking territories mainly abide by Spanish naming customs, though discrete surnames are usually joined with the word *i* ("and") instead of the Spanish *y*; this practice is very common in formal contexts. For example, the former [president of the Generalitat de Catalunya](/wiki/President_of_the_Generalitat_de_Catalunya "President of the Generalitat de Catalunya") (Government of Catalonia) is formally called *El Molt Honorable Senyor [Pere Aragonès i Garcia](/wiki/Pere_Aragon%C3%A8s_i_Garcia "Pere Aragonès i Garcia")*. The national language policy enumerated in article 19.1 of Law 1/1998 stipulates that "the citizens of Catalonia have the right to use the proper regulation of their Catalan names and surnames and to introduce the conjunction between surnames".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The correction, translation, and change of surnames are regulated by the *Registro Civil* (Civil Registry) in decree 138/2007 of 26 June, modifying decree 208/1998 of 30 July, which regulates the accreditation of the linguistic correctness of names. Decree 138/2007 of 26 July regulates the issuance of language-correction certificates for [translated](/wiki/Translation "Translation") Catalan names by the *[Institut d'Estudis Catalans](/wiki/Institut_d%27Estudis_Catalans "Institut d'Estudis Catalans")* (Institute of Catalan Studies) in Barcelona. Nevertheless, there are Catalan surnames that conform to neither the current spelling rules nor the traditionally correct Catalan [spelling](/wiki/Spelling "Spelling") rules; a language-correction certification can be requested from the institute, for names such as these:[[38]](#cite_note-40)

* Aleñá→Alenyà
* Caballé→Cavaller

* Casas→Cases

* Fábregas→Fàbregues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_naming_customs&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Catalan hypocoristics and nicknames")]

Many Catalan names are shortened to [hypocoristic](/wiki/Hypocoristic "Hypocoristic") forms using only the final portion of the name (unlike Spanish, which mostly uses only the first portion of the name), and with a diminutive suffix (*-et, -eta/-ita*). Thus, shortened Catalan names taking the first portion of the name are probably influenced by the Spanish tradition. The influence of Spanish in hypocoristics is recent since it became a general fashion only in the twentieth century and especially since [Francisco Franco](/wiki/Francisco_Franco "Francisco Franco")'s dictatorship[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]; example Catalan names are:

### Galician names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_naming_customs&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Galician names")]

The [Galician](/wiki/Galician_language "Galician language")-speaking areas also abide by the Spanish naming customs. The main differences are the usage of Galician given names and surnames.

#### Galician surnames

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_naming_customs&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Galician surnames")]

Further information: [Category:Galician-language surnames](/wiki/Category:Galician-language_surnames "Category:Galician-language surnames")

Most Galician surnames have their origin in local [toponymies](/wiki/Toponymy "Toponymy"), including Galician regions (**Salnés** < [Salnés](/wiki/Saln%C3%A9s "Salnés"), [Carnota](/wiki/Carnota "Carnota"), [Bergantiños](/wiki/Berganti%C3%B1os "Bergantiños")), towns ([Ferrol](/wiki/Ferrol,_Galicia "Ferrol, Galicia"), [Noia](/wiki/Noia "Noia")), parishes or villages (as [Andrade](/wiki/Andrade "Andrade")). Just like elsewhere, many surnames were also generated from jobs or professions (**Carpinteiro** 'carpenter', **Cabaleiro** 'Knight', **Ferreiro** 'Smith', **Besteiro** 'Crossbowman'), physical characteristics (**Gago** 'Twangy', **Tato** 'Stutterer', **Couceiro** 'Tall and thin', **Bugallo** 'fat', **Pardo** 'Swarthy'), or origin of the person (**Franco** and **Francés** 'French', **Portugués** 'Portuguese').

Although many Galician surnames have historically been adapted to Spanish phonetics and orthography, they are still clearly recognizable as Galician words: **Freijedo**, Spanish adaptation of *freixedo* 'place with ash-trees'; **Seijo** from *seixo* 'stone'; **Doval** from *do Val* 'of the Valley'; **Rejenjo** from *Reguengo*, Galician evolution of local Latin-Germanic word *Regalingo* 'Royal property'.

Specially relevant are the Galician surnames that originated from medieval [patronymics](/wiki/Patronymics "Patronymics"), present in local documentation since the 9th century, and popularized from the 12th century on. Although many of them were historically adapted to Spanish orthography,[[39]](#cite_note-41) phonetics and traditions, many are still characteristically Galician; the most common ones are:

* **Alonso** (medieval form *Afonso*, from the latinicized Germanic name *Adefonsus*).
* **Álvarez** (from medieval Alvares, from the Germanic name *Halvar(d)*, latinicized as *Alvarus*).
* **Ares** (from the name *Arias* or the town of [Ares](/wiki/Ares,_Spain "Ares, Spain")).
* **Bermúdez** (medieval form *Vermues*, from the latinicized Germanic name *Veremodus* + suffix -ici-).
* **Bernárdez** (from the Frankish name *Bernard* + suffix -ici-).
* **Vieitez, Vieites** (from the name *Bieito*, from Latin *Benedictus* + suffix -ici-).
* **Diz, Díaz** (from the name Didacus + suffix -ici-).
* **Domínguez** (medieval form *Domingues*, derived of the name *Domingo*, from Dominicus, + suffix -ici-).
* **Enríquez** (medieval form *Anrriques*, from the Frankish name *Henric* + suffix -ici-).
* **Estévez** (medieval form *Esteves*, from the name *Estevo*, derived of *Stephanus* + suffix -ici-).
* **Fernández** (medieval form *Fernandes*, from the name *Fernando*, derived from the Germanic name *Fredenandus* + suffix -ici-).
* **Froiz** (medieval form *Froaz*, from the Germanic name *Froila* 'Lord' + suffix -ici-).
* **García** (medieval form *Garçia*, from the name *Garcia*).
* **Giance** (from the name *Xian*, old orthography *Jiam*, derived of Latin *Iulianus* + suffix -ici-).
* **Gómez** (medieval form *Gomes*, from the name *Gomes*).
* **González** (medieval form *Gonçalves*, from the latinicized Germanic name *Gundisalvus* + suffix -ici-).
* **López** (medieval form *Lopes*, from the Latin nickname *Lupus* 'wolf').
* **Lourenzo, Lorenzo** (medieval form *Lourenço*, from the Latin name *Laurentius*).
* **Martínez, Martín, Martís** (from the Latin name *Martinus* + suffix -ici-).
* **Méndez** (medieval form *Meendes*, from the name *Mendo*, from *Menendus* + suffix -ici-).
* **Miguéns** (from the name *Miguel*, derived of *Michael* + suffix -ici-).
* **Núñez** (medieval form *Nunes*, derived from the name *Nunnus* + suffix -ici-).
* **Paz, Paes, Pais** (from the name *Paio*, derived from *Pelagius* + suffix -ici-).
* **Pérez** (medieval form *Peres*, from the name *Pero*, derived of *Petrus*, + suffix -ici-).
* **Raimúndez** (from the Frankish name *Raimund* + suffix -ici-).
* **Rodríguez** (from the name *Rodrigo*, from the latinicized Germanic form *Rodericus* + suffix -ici-).
* **Rois** (from the name *Roi*, nickname of *Rodrigo* + suffix -ici-): Spanish 'Ruiz'.
* **Sánchez** (medieval form *Sanches*, from the name *Sancho*, derived from Latin Sanctius + suffix -ici-).
* **Sueiro, Suárez** (medieval forms *Sueiro, Suares*, from the name *Suarius*, with and without suffix -ici-).
* **Vázquez** (medieval form *Vasques*, from the name *Vasco*, from *Velasco*, + suffix -ici-).
* **Yanes** (medieval forms *Eanes*, *Ianes*. from *Iohannes,* Yohannes + suffix -ici-).

Some of them (namely Páez, Méndez, Vázquez) show characteristic Galician dropping of intervocalic -l-, -d-, -g- and -n- (although [Lugo](/wiki/Lugo "Lugo") is the only province in Spain with a majority of people surnamed *López*).

#### Galician given names and nicknames

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_naming_customs&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Galician given names and nicknames")]

Some common Galician names are:[[40]](#cite_note-42)

* *Afonso* [m]: nicknames *Fonso*, *Pocho*.
* *Alberte* [m] *Alberta* [f]: *Berto*, *Berta*.
* *Alexandre* [m]: *Xandre*, *Álex*.
* *Anxo* [m]: *Xeluco*.
* *Antón* [m], *Antía* [f]: *Tonecho*.
* *Artai* [m].
* *Baldomero* [m]: *Mero*
* *Bieito* [m], *Bieita* [f]
* *Brais* [m]

Nicknames are usually obtained from the end of a given name, or through derivation. Common suffixes include masculine -iño, -ito (as in *Sito*, from *Luisito*), -echo (*Tonecho*, from *Antonecho*) and -uco (*Farruco*, from *Francisco*); and feminine -iña, -ucha/uxa (*Maruxa*, *Carmucha*, from *Maria* and *Carme*), -uca (*Beluca*, from *Isabeluca*), and -ela (*Mela*, from *Carmela*).

### Ceuta and Melilla

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_naming_customs&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Ceuta and Melilla")]

As the provincial *Surname distribution* map (above) indicates, [Mohamed](/wiki/Muhammad_(name) "Muhammad (name)") is an often-occurring [surname](/wiki/Surname "Surname") in the autonomous Mediterranean North African cities of [Ceuta](/wiki/Ceuta "Ceuta") and [Melilla](/wiki/Melilla "Melilla") (respectively registered 10,410 and 7,982 occurrences),[[41]](#cite_note-INE-43) [Hispanophone](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language") Muslims use the Spanish "Mohamed" spelling for "[Muhammad](/wiki/Muhammad "Muhammad")". As such, it is often a component of [Arabic names](/wiki/Arabic_name "Arabic name") for men; hence, many Ceutan and Melillan Muslims share surnames despite not sharing a common ancestry. Furthermore, *Mohamed* (Muhammad) is the most popular name for new-born boys,[[42]](#cite_note-INEExcel-44) thus it is not unusual to encounter a man named *Mohamed Mohamed Mohamed*: the first occurrence is the given name, the second occurrence is the paternal surname, and the third occurrence is the maternal surname.[[43]](#cite_note-45)

## Indexing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_naming_customs&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Indexing")]

In English, the [Chicago Manual of Style](/wiki/Chicago_Manual_of_Style "Chicago Manual of Style") recommends that Spanish and Hispanophone names be [indexed](/wiki/Subject_indexing "Subject indexing") by the family name. When there are two family names, the indexing is done under the father's family name; this would be the first element of the surname if the father's and mother's or husband's family names are joined by a *y*. Depending upon the person involved, the particle *de* may be treated as a part of a family name or it may be separated from a family name. The indexing of Hispanophone names differs from that of Portuguese or [Lusophone names](/wiki/Portuguese_name "Portuguese name"), where the final element of the name is indexed because the Portuguese custom is for the father's surname to follow, rather than precede, the mother's. The effect is that the father's surname is the one indexed for both Spanish and Portuguese names.[[44]](#cite_note-46)
